title: Chapter 17: Electrical Circuits and Electrical Safety — Hard

questions.question: A potential divider consists of a $100\text{ }Omega$ fixed resistor and a thermistor connected to a $6\text{ V}$ source. At $0^\circ\text{C}$, the thermistor resistance is $500\text{ }Omega$. What is the output voltage across the $100\text{ }Omega$ resistor?
questions.explanation:
Using the potential divider formula: $V_{out} = (R_1 / (R_1 + R_2)) \times V_{in} = (100 / (100 + 500)) \times 6 = (100 / 600) \times 6 = 1.0\text{ V}$. 

**Common mistake:** Calculating the voltage across the thermistor ($5.0\text{ V}$) instead of the resistor.

questions.question: A circuit contains a battery and an LED. If the battery's terminals are reversed, what will happen to the LED?
options: The LED will not light up because it is now reverse-biased.
The LED will light up with a different colour.
The LED will become much brighter before burning out.
The LED will continue to function normally.
questions.explanation:
An LED (Light-Emitting Diode) only conducts and emits light when forward-biased. Reversing the terminals prevents current from flowing. 

**Common mistake:** Thinking the LED is like a filament lamp, which works regardless of direction.

questions.question: How does the 'rectified' current produced by a diode in an a.c. circuit compare to a standard direct current (d.c.) from a battery?
options: The rectified current is periodic and intermittent, whereas d.c. is steady.
The rectified current is much stronger than battery d.c.
They are identical in every way.
The rectified current still reverses direction every half cycle.
questions.explanation:
Standard d.c. provides a constant flow, while a single diode in an a.c. circuit produces pulses of current in one direction with gaps in between. 

**Common mistake:** Thinking 'rectified' means the current is no longer periodic.

questions.question: A battery provides $0.6\text{ J}$ of energy to drive $0.4\text{ C}$ of charge around a complete circuit. Calculate the electromotive force (e.m.f.) of the battery.
questions.explanation:
Using $E = W / Q$, the e.m.f. is $0.6 / 0.4 = 1.5\text{ V}$. (Matches logic in Worked Example 16B). 

**Common mistake:** Multiplying the values ($0.6 \times 0.4$) to get $0.24$.

questions.question: Which of the following would cause the gradient of an $I-V$ graph for a metallic conductor to stay constant?
options: Keeping the temperature of the conductor constant
Increasing the potential difference at a very high rate
Using a thinner wire of the same material
Replacing the conductor with a semiconductor diode
questions.explanation:
A constant gradient indicates constant resistance (Ohmic behavior). Metals typically only show this if their temperature remains steady. 

**Common mistake:** Thinking that 'any' metal conductor always has a constant gradient.

questions.question: Three resistors ($2\text{ }Omega, 4\text{ }Omega, 6\text{ }Omega$) are connected in series with a $12\text{ V}$ battery. Calculate the power dissipated by the $4\text{ }Omega$ resistor.
questions.explanation:
Total $R = 2 + 4 + 6 = 12\text{ }Omega$. Current $I = 12 / 12 = 1\text{ A}$. Power $P = I^2R$ (or $IV$). P.d. across $4\text{ }Omega$ is $1 \times 4 = 4\text{ V}$. $P = 1\text{ A} \times 4\text{ V} = 4.0\text{ W}$.

**Common mistake:** Review the method above and check units/signs.

questions.question: A series circuit consists of a source and two resistors. Resistor A has twice the resistance of Resistor B. What is the ratio of the potential difference across A to that across B?
questions.explanation:
In series, $V_A = IR_A$ and $V_B = IR_B$. Since the current $I$ is the same, $V_A / V_B = R_A / R_B$. If $R_A = 2R_B$, then $V_A = 2V_B$, giving a $2 : 1$ ratio.

**Common mistake:** Review the method above and check units/signs.

questions.question: A student adds a second identical battery in series to a circuit containing a single lamp. How does this affect the current in the circuit and the brightness of the lamp?
options: Current increases and brightness remains same
Current remains same and brightness decreases
questions.explanation:
Adding batteries in series increases the total e.m.f. ($V_{total} = V_1 + V_2$). Since $I = V_{total} / R$, the current increases, making the lamp brighter.

**Common mistake:** Review the method above and check units/signs.

questions.question: A potential divider consists of two resistors, $R_1 = 200\text{ }Omega$ and $R_2 = 600\text{ }Omega$, connected in series with a $12\text{ V}$ source. What is the output voltage measured across $R_2$?
questions.explanation:
Using the potential divider formula: $V_{out} = [R_2 / (R_1 + R_2)] \times V_{in} = [600 / (200 + 600)] \times 12 = 0.75 \times 12 = 9\text{ V}$.

**Common mistake:** Review the method above and check units/signs.

questions.question: In a series circuit, the total resistance is $50\text{ }Omega$. When an additional resistor of $10\text{ }Omega$ is added in series, the current drops by $0.1\text{ A}$. What was the original e.m.f. of the source?
questions.explanation:
Original: $I_1 = V / 50$. New: $I_2 = V / 60$. We know $I_1 - I_2 = 0.1$. So $V/50 - V/60 = 0.1 rightarrow (6V - 5V) / 300 = 0.1 rightarrow V / 300 = 0.1$, so $V = 30\text{ V}$.

**Common mistake:** Review the method above and check units/signs.

questions.question: A circuit contains three resistors in parallel: $4\text{ }Omega$, $6\text{ }Omega$, and $12\text{ }Omega$. Calculate the combined resistance of this arrangement.
questions.explanation:
$1/R = 1/4 + 1/6 + 1/12 = 3/12 + 2/12 + 1/12 = 6/12 = 1/2$. Thus $R = 2\text{ }Omega$. 

**Common mistake:** Calculating the reciprocal but forgetting to invert it at the end.

questions.question: A $5\text{ V}$ cell is connected to two resistors in parallel. The current in the first branch is $0.1\text{ A}$ and the total current from the cell is $0.4\text{ A}$. Determine the resistance of the second resistor.
questions.explanation:
Current in second branch $I_2 = I_{total} - I_1 = 0.4 - 0.1 = 0.3\text{ A}$. Since $V=5\text{V}$, $R_2 = 5 / 0.3 \approx 16.7\text{ }Omega$. (Based on logic in Practise 17.2/3 Q1). 

**Common mistake:** Using the total current to find the resistance ($5/0.4 = 12.5$).

questions.question: In a complex parallel circuit, branch 1 has resistance $R$ and branch 2 has resistance $2R$. If an ammeter in the main circuit reads $0.9\text{ A}$, what is the current in branch 2?
questions.explanation:
Current splits inversely to resistance. $I_1 = 2I_2$. Since $I_1 + I_2 = 0.9$, then $3I_2 = 0.9 implies I_2 = 0.3\text{ A}$. 

**Common mistake:** Thinking the branch with $2R$ gets double the current ($0.6\text{ A}$).

questions.question: Three identical resistors are connected in parallel to a battery. If one resistor is removed from the circuit, what happens to the total current and the total resistance?
options: Total current decreases; total resistance increases.
Total current increases; total resistance decreases.
Total current decreases; total resistance decreases.
Both remain the same because the voltage is constant.
questions.explanation:
Removing a parallel branch increases the combined resistance (fewer paths). Since $I = V/R$, and $V$ is constant, the total current decreases. 

**Common mistake:** Thinking removing a component always decreases resistance.

questions.question: A $12\text{ V}$ battery is connected to a parallel combination of $100\text{ }Omega$ and $400\text{ }Omega$. Calculate the combined resistance if a third $100\text{ }Omega$ resistor is added in parallel to the first two.
questions.explanation:
$1/R = 1/100 + 1/400 + 1/100 = 4/400 + 1/400 + 4/400 = 9/400$. $R = 400/9 \approx 44.4\text{ }Omega$. 

**Common mistake:** Adding the third resistor in series to the parallel pair.

questions.question: A potential divider contains a thermistor and a $100\text{ }Omega$ fixed resistor connected to a $6\text{ V}$ cell. The thermistor resistance is $500\text{ }Omega$ at $0^\circ\text{C}$ and $50\text{ }Omega$ at $100^\circ\text{C}$. What is the voltmeter reading across the thermistor at $100^\circ\text{C}$?
questions.explanation:
At $100^\circ\text{C}$, $R_{therm} = 50\text{ }Omega$ and $R_{fixed} = 100\text{ }Omega$. Total $R = 150\text{ }Omega$. $V_{out} = (50 / 150) \times 6 = 1/3 \times 6 = 2.0\text{ V}$. (Note: The Practice 17.4 q1 in the source asks for the reading across the fixed resistor, which would be $4.0\text{ V}$).

**Common mistake:** Review the method above and check units/signs.

questions.question: A potential divider has a variable resistor $R_1$ (range $0\text{--}4\text{ }Omega$) and a fixed resistor $R_2 = 6\text{ }Omega$ connected to a $12\text{ V}$ source. What are the minimum and maximum possible values of $V_{out}$ measured across $R_2$?
questions.explanation:
Max $V_{out}$ is when $R_1 = 0$: $V = (6/6) \times 12 = 12\text{ V}$. Min $V_{out}$ is when $R_1 = 4$: $V = [6 / (4+6)] \times 12 = 0.6 \times 12 = 7.2\text{ V}$. This matches logic from Review Q9.

**Common mistake:** Review the method above and check units/signs.

questions.question: A student wants to design a circuit that turns on a lamp when the temperature falls below a certain level. Which arrangement of a thermistor and a relay would be most appropriate?
options: Thermistor in a potential divider to activate a relay when its resistance is high
Thermistor in a potential divider to activate a relay when its resistance is low
Thermistor connected in series with the lamp and a d.c. supply
Thermistor used as a fuse to break the circuit when it gets too cold
questions.explanation:
When it gets cold, a thermistor's resistance increases. A potential divider can be used to provide enough voltage to a relay to switch on a secondary high-power lamp circuit once that resistance threshold is reached.

**Common mistake:** Review the method above and check units/signs.

questions.question: A potential divider uses a $9\text{ V}$ source, a $24\text{ }Omega$ fixed resistor, and a variable resistor ($0\text{--}12\text{ }Omega$). To obtain a $V_{out}$ that can vary from $0\text{ V}$ to $3\text{ V}$, how should the variable resistor be connected?
options: Measure $V_{out}$ across the variable resistor which is in series with the fixed resistor
Measure $V_{out}$ across the fixed resistor which is in series with the variable resistor
Connect both resistors in parallel with the voltmeter
The desired range is impossible with these components
questions.explanation:
If $V_{out}$ is across the variable resistor $R_{var}$: at $0\text{ }Omega, V_{out} = 0\text{ V}$. At $12\text{ }Omega, V_{out} = [12 / (12 + 24)] \times 9 = (12/36) \times 9 = 1/3 \times 9 = 3\text{ V}$. This confirms the design from Review Section B Q5.

**Common mistake:** Review the method above and check units/signs.

questions.question: A $12\text{ V}$ supply is connected to a $500\text{ k}Omega$ resistor and an LDR in series. In the dark, the LDR resistance is $1\text{ M}Omega$. What is the potential difference across the LDR in the dark?
questions.explanation:
$1\text{ M}Omega = 1000\text{ k}Omega$. Using the formula: $V_{LDR} = [1000 / (500 + 1000)] \times 12 = (1000 / 1500) \times 12 = 2/3 \times 12 = 8.0\text{ V}$. This matches the logic for Review Q10.

**Common mistake:** Review the method above and check units/signs.

questions.question: An appliance is rated at $2500\text{ W}$ and is connected to a $230\text{ V}$ supply. If the available fuse ratings are $3\text{ A}$, $5\text{ A}$, $10\text{ A}$, and $13\text{ A}$, which fuse should be selected?
questions.explanation:
Operating current $I = P / V = 2500 / 230 \approx 10.87\text{ A}$. The fuse must be rated higher than this, so the $13\text{ A}$ fuse is the only safe choice. 

**Common mistake:** Choosing $10\text{ A}$, which would blow as soon as the appliance is turned on.

questions.question: If a fault occurs where the live wire touches the metal casing of an earthed appliance, what sequence of events protects the user?
options: A large current flows through the earth wire, causing the fuse to blow and isolating the appliance.
The earth wire increases its resistance, pushing the current back to the power station.
The neutral wire becomes live to balance the potential difference.
The plastic insulation on the wires melts to seal the leak.
questions.explanation:
The earth wire provides a low-resistance path, creating a very large current that exceeds the fuse rating. The fuse then melts, cutting off the electricity. 

**Common mistake:** Thinking the earth wire 'absorbs' the shock without blowing the fuse.

questions.question: Explain why a switch placed in the neutral wire is dangerous, even when the switch is 'open' (off).
options: The internal components of the appliance remain at a high potential (live).
The appliance will continue to draw its full operating power.
The earth wire will begin to carry the full return current.
The fuse will blow because the potential difference has nowhere to go.
questions.explanation:
Breaking the neutral path stops current flow, but the live wire is still connected to the appliance's internals, keeping them at $230\text{ V}$. Touching them would complete a circuit to the ground through the person. 

**Common mistake:** Thinking 'open' always means 'safe' regardless of location.

questions.question: A domestic circuit is protected by a $30\text{ A}$ trip switch. If five $2.0\text{ kW}$ kettles are plugged into this circuit ($V = 240\text{ V}$), what will happen?
options: The total current will be approximately $41.7\text{ A}$, and the trip switch will flip.
The kettles will each operate at $1/5$ of their rated power.
The current will be $25\text{ A}$, and the circuit will function normally.
The voltage will drop to $48\text{ V}$ to keep the current safe.
questions.explanation:
Total power $P = 5 \times 2000 = 10000\text{ W}$. Total current $I = 10000 / 240 \approx 41.7\text{ A}$. Since $41.7 > 30$, the breaker will trip. 

**Common mistake:** Assuming appliances in a house are in series and share voltage.

questions.question: Which combination of safety features is essential for a high-power metal-cased washing machine?
options: A fuse in the live wire and a connected earth wire.
A fuse in the neutral wire and double insulation.
A diode to rectify the current and a variable resistor.
An ammeter in series and a voltmeter in parallel.
questions.explanation:
Metal-cased appliances require earthing to prevent the case from becoming live and a fuse in the live wire to break the circuit during a fault. 

**Common mistake:** Thinking a voltmeter is a safety feature.
